skill.name: deployment-pipeline-design
skill.description: Design secure multi-stage CI/CD pipelines with approval gates, deployment strategies, rollback paths, and operational verification.
skill.summary: Plan CI/CD pipelines with staged controls, deployment gates, rollbacks, and release verification.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer correctly detected command, network, and secret-like patterns, but review shows they are documentation examples in SKILL.md rather than executable skill code. No prompt injection, obfuscation, or malicious exfiltration intent was found. Publish with a medium warning because copied pipeline examples can affect production infrastructure if used without review.

medium_findings.title: Production Deployment Command Examples Require Review
medium_findings.description: The confirmed command patterns are CI/CD documentation snippets, not runtime code executed by the skill. They include kubectl, docker, make, trivy, npm, curl, and rollback commands that can alter infrastructure if copied into a live pipeline without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly present in deployment examples, so the pattern is real. Confidence is below very high because the skill itself does not execute the commands.

medium_findings.title: External Endpoint and Secret Placeholder Examples Need Validation
medium_findings.description: The examples include placeholder application URLs, a Prometheus query URL, an API key placeholder, and a GitHub Actions secret reference. These are normal for pipeline design, but users must replace them with approved endpoints and secret stores.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show network and secret-like examples with legitimate deployment context. There is no evidence that the skill reads or transmits real local secrets.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby or Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many static external command hits are Markdown code fences or inline file references. They are not executable skill logic and do not create command execution by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited patterns are Markdown delimiters or inline code markers. They do not invoke an interpreter or execute commands inside the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Text-Match False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography blocker findings point to frontmatter, headings, descriptions, and ordinary wording. No cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic API appears at the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain metadata and prose about deployment pipeline design. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Placeholders
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings use app.example.com in sample pipelines. These are placeholder endpoints, not a hidden outbound call made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example.com domain is reserved for examples and appears inside YAML snippets. The context supports documentation use rather than exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: The heuristic combined command examples, URLs, and an API key placeholder across a Markdown guide. No evidence found for obfuscation, malicious credential access, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky primitives are present only as visible CI/CD examples. The file contains no hidden executable wrapper, encoded payload, or instruction to bypass security review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mutable Infrastructure Commands in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes commands that deploy, promote, label, or roll back Kubernetes resources. These commands are expected for the domain but require environment-specific review before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and operational, so the risk exists if copied blindly. The visible Markdown context makes malicious execution unlikely.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Monitoring Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The monitoring example uses shell command substitution with curl, jq, echo, and bc. This is a legitimate pipeline pattern, but production use should validate inputs and quote variables carefully.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static command-substitution findings are real in the example. There is no direct evidence of user-controlled command injection in the skill itself.

content.value_statement: Release pipelines often grow without clear gates, rollback plans, or verification steps. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design structured CI/CD workflows for safer delivery.
actual_capabilities: Defines standard CI/CD stages from source checkout through production verification.
Explains manual, delayed, and multi-approver deployment gate patterns.
Compares rolling, blue-green, canary, and feature flag release strategies.
Provides example GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Azure Pipelines, and Kubernetes snippets.
Lists rollback, monitoring, and delivery metrics for production readiness.
limitations: Does not execute, test, or validate pipeline commands automatically.
Examples use placeholders that must be adapted to real repositories and environments.
Does not replace cloud provider, compliance, or platform-specific deployment review.
Referenced supplemental files may be unavailable in the published package.

use_cases.title: Plan a Production Release Flow
use_cases.description: Design a staged pipeline with tests, approval gates, progressive rollout, verification, and rollback steps.

use_cases.title: Standardize Platform Deployment Patterns
use_cases.description: Compare rolling, canary, blue-green, and feature flag strategies for shared delivery standards.

use_cases.title: Add Safer Gates to Existing CI/CD
use_cases.description: Identify where manual approvals, delayed releases, security scans, and monitoring checks should be added.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CI/CD pipeline for my application. Include build, test, staging, approval, production, verification, and rollback stages.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Deployment Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare rolling, blue-green, canary, and feature flag deployment for my service. Recommend one based on risk, traffic, and rollback needs.

prompt_templates.title: Add Approval and Security Gates
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this deployment flow and add approval gates, security checks, artifact controls, and monitoring checks before production.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Advanced GitOps Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitOps deployment pipeline for multiple environments with progressive delivery, policy checks, automated rollback, and release metrics.

output_examples.input: Design a production pipeline for a web API.
output_examples.output: A staged release plan with build, unit tests, container scan, staging deployment, smoke tests, manual approval, canary production rollout, health checks, and rollback criteria.

output_examples.input: Help choose between blue-green and canary deployment.
output_examples.output: A comparison of operational cost, rollback speed, user exposure, observability requirements, and a recommendation based on service risk.

output_examples.input: Add rollback planning to our existing pipeline.
output_examples.output: A rollback checklist covering deployment history, health thresholds, automated failure handling, manual recovery commands, and post-incident metrics.

best_practices: Replace every placeholder endpoint, secret, and command with environment-specific values before use.
Add verification gates that measure health, error rates, and rollback readiness after every production change.
Keep deployment approval rules, ownership, and rollback criteria documented beside the pipeline definition.
anti_patterns: Do not copy sample production commands into a live pipeline without review.
Do not store real API keys, webhooks, or tokens directly in pipeline files.
Do not rely on manual approval as the only control before production deployment.

faq.question: Does this skill run deployment commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides design guidance and examples. Users must run and verify commands in their own environments.

faq.question: Can it create GitHub Actions or GitLab CI workflows?
faq.answer: It can outline workflow structures and provide example snippets for those systems.

faq.question: Does it handle secrets management?
faq.answer: It recommends using secret stores and platform secret references, but it does not manage secrets directly.

faq.question: Is it suitable for regulated environments?
faq.answer: It can support planning, but compliance controls need separate legal, security, and platform review.

faq.question: Which deployment strategies does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers rolling, blue-green, canary, feature flag, manual approval, delayed approval, and multi-approver patterns.

faq.question: What should I provide before using it?
faq.answer: Provide your platform, environments, risk tolerance, test strategy, approval needs, and rollback requirements.
